root_cause_candidates.cause: Memory fault detected in ts-auth-service (mem_jump > 0.3) with elevated CPU, propagating requests to downstream services including ts-preserve-service, leading to downstream symptoms in preserve service.
root_cause_candidates.reasoning: The Log Agent identifies ts-auth-service as the deeper fault with mem_jump > 0.3 and concurrently notes downstream impact on ts-preserve-service. Although the Topology Agent reports no direct path from ts-auth-service to ts-preserve-service in the provided dependency graph, the log-based propagation signal is strong enough to elevate ts-auth-service as the root cause, and the TCB-RCA baseline contradicts this by not mapping a feasible path. Given the principle to override topology when log evidence is strong, ts-auth-service is favored as the root cause. The most plausible propagation is ts-auth-service -> ts-preserve-service.

root_cause_candidates.cause: Self-contained fault or degradation within ts-preserve-service (the symptom service) with no explicit upstream propagation path supported by topology, suggesting either an internal issue or a local resource constraint.
root_cause_candidates.reasoning: TCB-RCA places ts-preserve-service at depth 0 with no temporal gap, indicating no proven upstream propagation within the measured graph. The Topology Agent explicitly deems the propagation path implausible from ts-auth-service to ts-preserve-service, undermining ts-auth-service as the root under structural constraints. The Log Agent notes ts-preserve-service as a downstream symptom and highlights ts-auth-service as a stronger upstream signal, but the absence of a supported path in topology makes ts-preserve-service a plausible local root cause or an isolated fault in preservation service. This candidate captures the possibility of a weakly supported internal issue in the symptom service.

metadata.hypothesis: ts-auth-service experiences memory fault (mem_jump > 0.3) which propagates requests to dependent services, including ts-preserve-service, causing downstream symptoms in the preserve service.
metadata.llm_reasoning: Metric signals identify ts-auth-service as a strong root-cause candidate (mem_jump > 0.3, cpu_z elevated). ts-preserve-service shows milder metric anomalies and is the symptom service downstream of ts-auth-service. The topology indicates ts-preserve-service depends on ts-auth-service, so the deeper root cause is likely ts-auth-service. Log-rate signals show downstream impact, but no other service exhibits stronger metrics that would contradict ts-auth-service as the primary fault.

result.synthesis_reasoning: All three sources diverge on the root cause. Log Agent strongly implicates ts-auth-service as the upstream fault with mem-jump indicating memory stress and a downstream impact on ts-preserve-service. The Topology Agent finds no structural path from ts-auth-service to ts-preserve-service, arguing against a direct propagation link and suggests ts-preserve-service as the only contained blast radius. The TCB-RCA baseline marks ts-preserve-service as the root with zero temporal gap, but this contradicts the log-run inference of an upstream fault. Given the rule to prioritize logs for resource-driven cascades and to respect topology unless log evidence is compelling, the most credible single root candidate is ts-auth-service. The most plausible propagation path is ts-auth-service -> ts-preserve-service, with ts-preserve-service possibly exhibiting local degradation or acting as the symptom amplifier. If further data becomes available (e.g., ts-auth-service metrics over time, cross-service traces, or deeper resource diagnostics in ts-preserve-service), this assessment could be updated to either reinforce ts-auth-service as root or reclassify ts-preserve-service as the primary fault.
